essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 1.1 : Whole Number - Saying Number Sequence, Meaningful Counting and Skip Counting

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Count by 1s, 2s, 5s and 10s, forward and backward, to 100.
Say number sequence by 1s, 2s, 5s and 10s, forward and backward, starting from any point, to 100.
Read, write and represent whole numbers from 1 - 100.
Demonstrate different ways of representing numbers from 1 - 100.
Skip count orally in 2s, 5s and 10s up to 200.
Use a variety of methods to count and skip count.
Volunteer to count/skip count up to a given number of real-life objects found in the classroom.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Observation
Concrete: Check to see whether students can use manipulatives to count and build quantities up to 100.
Pictorial: Observe students as they match pictorial representation of quantities with the correct number up to 100.

Symbolic: Listen to students as they call out numerals based on a representation shown of a particular quantity up to 100. Observe students write the numeral in figures and words after being shown a representation of a particular quantity up to 100.
Think, pair, share: Have students select a card of their choice then pair with each other to match the symbolic representation of the quantity shown on their card.
Observation: Allow students to draw quantities to represent a given amount up to 100. Listen to students as they count in sequence to 100.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Provide opportunities for students to experience counting in 1s, 2s, 5s and 10s, to 100 in engaging in real life situations. For example, through games, songs, and local stories. Students should begin counting initially from 1- 100 or any given number using a counting chart and number lines.
Encourage students to ‘build’ numbers using concrete manipulatives – include the ten frames and beaded number lines. Explore the numbers and find the different variations using number bonds.

Let students represent numbers from 1-100 using concrete manipulatives (coloured tiles, counters, counting sticks, cubes, marbles, toothpicks, buttons, corks, square tiles etc.) to count and build quantities to 100 and by pictorial representation. For example, have students fill small containers with a specified number of objects.

Have students create visuals to represent counting by 2s, 5s, and 10s. e.g., skip counting by 5s could be represented by pictures of each student’s hand with the fingers spread apart, or counting by 2s could be represented by pictures of each student’s eyes, etc.
Provide students with a number of counters (e.g. 50), and allow them to separate the counters from the pile as they count them by groups of 2s, 5s or 10s.
Provide opportunities for students to read and write numbers from 1 - 100.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 1.2 : Whole Number - Representing and Partitioning Quantities

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Distinguish between odd and even numbers for groups with up to 20 objects.
Identify equations that express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
Write an equation to express an even number as
a sum of two numbers of equal value.

Values
• Willingly participate in activities involving odd and even numbers.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Students compete in pairs or teams to differentiate among given 2-digit numbers. Each team is given response cards with the words ‘odd’ and ‘even’. As numbers are displayed, the first pair/team that indicates the correct type of number, scores the point. The pair/team with the highest score is the winner.

Students line up in teams. Two large containers (e.g. buckets) are labelled ‘ODD’ and ‘EVEN’ and placed at the front of each line. Each team is given cards with various 2 digit numbers. Team members are instructed to correctly fill each bucket with odd and even numbers. The team with the most correct answers is the winner.
Students place number values on a sheet of paper to determine if they are even or odd.
Students will share their observations about the numbers that are odd or even based on these activities.
Worksheet : Students identify odd and even numbers.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Guided discovery - Identify odd and even numbers up to 20.

Have students use square tiles to make equal rows and have them identify which number makes equal rows and which do not. This will help students to sort a given set of numbers as even or odd. E.g., 7 groups of 2 with one left over or 2 equal groups with one left over.
Ask students to count a certain number of objects. They will then group them in twos. They will do this with different numbers and note their observations. They can look for any pattern
in the numbers. Separate the numbers based on their observations.

Let children work with numbers and take out a certain number of objects and if that number is an even number, they run to the side that says even, but if the number is an odd number, they run to the side that says odd.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 1.3: Whole Number - Comparing and ordering quantities

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Compare and order numbers up to 100 in ascending or descending order.
Indicate and describe the position of specific
numbers in a sequence by using ordinal numbers up to tenth.
Identify 2-digit numbers.
Use vocabulary related to comparing numbers (greater than, less than, equal to).
Distinguish among the symbols (<, >, and =) used to compare 2-digit numbers.
Skills: Arrange numbers in order of size and give reasons for their arrangement.
Use relevant vocabulary to orally compare numbers (greater than, less than, equal to).
Compare pairs of 2-digit numbers using the symbols (<, > and =).
Values: Show willingness to participate in games and other activities involving comparing numbers.
Volunteer to share on how to compare pairs of numbers.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Worksheet
Students will complete a worksheet on comparing numbers using the symbols (<, >, =)
Observation: Observe students as they use concrete materials to represent given quantities in order to compare 2-digit numbers. Listen as students compare numbers using related vocabulary.
Think Pair Share: Students work in pairs to complete a worksheet by placing the correct number in each box.
Online game
https://wordwall.net/resource/48323381
https://wordwall.net/resource/37166546
inclusive_learning_strategies: Using concrete materials

Provide opportunity for students to use concrete objects to compare pairs of numbers, using the symbols < , >, and = to compare numbers or using terms such as ‘more than’, ‘less than’ or ‘the same as’

Games: Have students play a game by grabbing items and comparing to see who has more. Let students use comparative language to describe a given number (e.g., thirty-six is four less than forty or forty is four more than thirty-six). Also, allow them to arrange sets of numbers in order, beginning from the smallest or vice versa.

Through play have students observe and talk about positions in line-ups. E.g. Who is third? If there are 6 people in front of you, where are you in the line? If I move an individual two spaces forward or backward, what is my new position?
Provide students with opportunities to arrange numbers, pictures, etc. in a specific order then name the position of each. E.g. Which number is first, second or last?
Use daily routines to reinforce comparing and ordering. E.g. The first group to finish a task, the last person in the line, etc.
Play games to compare numbers using the symbols: < or >.

Math stories: Use stories to enable students to understand the difference among the concepts of less than, more than, and equal to. Example: Last Monday, Jack, Jill and Billy went to a farm to pick sapodillas. Jack picked 25, Jill picked 40 and Billy picked 25 sapodillas. a. Who picked more sapodillas? Jill or Billy? b. Which statement is true? 40 < 25, 40 > 25, 25 = 40
c. Which two friends picked the same number of sapodillas?

Students play a game of cards. Teacher deals the cards and students will put down cards not knowing the value. Students will see which card is bigger after putting them down. The person with the bigger/higher value card gets to keep both cards.

Provide the opportunity for students to compare pairs of numbers using tens and one base blocks. Students will use blocks to represent numbers then compare them using the terms and symbols associated with less than, more than, and equal to. For example: 15 is less than 25
Source: https://www.nagwa.com/en/worksheets/697151562636/

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 1.4: Whole Number - Place Value

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Identify and contrast among the face value, place value, and total value of the digits in any two-digit number.
Represent numbers concretely and pictorially up to ninety-nine (99) as groups of tens and ones.
Write the place value and total value of any digit in a 2-digit number.
Round two-digit numbers to the nearest 10
Willingly participate in games and other activities involving face value, place value, and total value of digits in 2-digit numbers.
Appreciate the importance of knowing the place value and total value of digits.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Students are given a number and asked to demonstrate the number 4 ways. Students will explain their work. (learners can make poems, stories, drawings, role play to represent the number).
Students complete worksheets.
Projects: Students create crafts demonstrating place value of numbers.

Questioning/Group activity: You are having a party and there will be twenty-six people there. You need to get enough plates to serve the food. Plates are usually sold in 10s. How many packs of plates might you have to buy?
inclusive_learning_strategies: Use explicit Teaching. Model writing number in expanded form and read it in words. Use guided questions for students to discover that the digits of a number express the values of their own when the number is given in expanded form and read in words. Hence, the value of a digit when expressed in expanded form of the number is called its place value in the number.

Provide opportunities for students to demonstrate that Place Value of a Digit = Digit × Position of digit. Use stories and games for students to distinguish between face value and place value. Use guided questions for students to discover that the face value of a digit is the digit itself, at whatever place it may be. The face value of a digit never changes. It is unchangeable and definite. But place value changes according to the digit’s place.
Allow students to use base ten blocks to understand the value of numbers.

Students play games selecting the correct number based on the place values given. Using the game helps to vary the strategy which is necessary to help students in the class who do not learn in the same way. It helps to keep the students engaged and on the concept being taught. This helps students to focus on the position of the numbers so that if 1 ten and 3 ones is shown students should not select 31.

Rounding to the nearest 10: Have Students look at a number chart to determine any number that is close to the next. For example, students will look at the numbers that are closest to 5 and then 10. They are likely to say 4 and 6 and 9 and 11 respectively. Other random numbers can be used. Students can then identify four numbers instead of two that are closest to a given number. This reinforces closest or nearest. To focus students more on multiples of 10, individual rows can be extracted from the number chart and represented as a number line. Students must understand that it is the same thing as the counting numbers but they are now on a line. Students will be given examples later where the middle poses a problem of not showing exactly where it is closest. Here the teacher will guide students to go for the higher multiple of 10. Emphasis must be placed on students’ counting ability in 10’s.
Using the number line

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 2.1: Fractions, Decimals and Rational Numbers - Representing Fraction

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Recognize simple fractions as representation of the parts of a whole. 1. Identify unit fractions correctly.
2. Distinguish between the numerator and the denominator.
Skills 1. Partition given shapes into parts with equal sections.
2. Shade parts of given shapes to represent named unit fractions up to eighths.
Values 1. Willingly collaborate with classmates in activities involving identifying fractions.
2. Choose to explain the use of fractions in their everyday lives.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Worksheet
Write the fraction that is shaded:
Source: https://superstarworksheets.com/math-worksheets/fractions-worksheets/
Source: https://ecdn.teacherspayteachers.com/thumbitem/Fraction-Poster-FREEBIE-1223841-1398921970/original-1223841-1.jpg
Shade each shape according to the fraction given.

Using manipulatives: Use playdough or items in the environment to show fractions. Students will explain what each fraction is showing. For example: Students will state what fraction the blue represents in the picture and what the purple part represents in each picture.
Oral Questioning: Question students about halves of different sized objects or liquid amounts. Students are encouraged to share their observations.
Project
Have students create a book/flower fraction using pictures or objects in their environment to depict unit fractions.
Students create a video making juice showing different fractions (students are expected to say the fractional parts they are using in the process of making the juice).
Students make a sandwich and cut it in halves or quarters to share.
Students should recognize the fraction that is shaded.
two sevenths
one third
three fifths
three quarters
five eighths
half
one quarter
Which of these shows 1/3?
inclusive_learning_strategies: Have students identify and partition whole objects. E.g. Cut fruits, divide shapes, or separate the people in a group/class, paper folding etc.

Allows students to experience, concretely and pictorially for example using pattern blocks or square tiles, that one-half is actually one out of two equal parts and one -fourth is actually one out of four equal parts.
Provide opportunities for students to represent fractional parts of a whole, particularly ½ and ¼, using concrete materials such as pattern blocks or square tiles and diagrams.
Have students folding strips of paper and other geometric shapes into two and four equal parts respectively.
Allow students to shade one out of two parts and name it one half, and one out of four parts and name it one quarter.

Provide opportunities for students to recognize and name one half of a whole which is divided into 2 equal parts. Students will also have the chance to identify and name one quarter of a whole which is divided into 4 equal parts.
Using manipulatives: Provide students with playdough or paper. Have them use it to show different unit fractions.
Using a fraction chart, students will compare and order fractions. They will state their observations about the size in comparison to other factional parts.
Students look at a read aloud about fractions to help connect fraction to real life situations.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome 2.2: Fractions, Decimals and Rational Numbers - Comparing and Ordering Fractions

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Knowledge: 1. Read and write the fractions ½ and ¼ correctly as ‘one half’ and ‘one quarter’ (Not ‘one over two’ and ‘one over four’).
Skills: 1. Compare two fractions with the same denominator (limited to halves and quarters) referring to the same whole.
Skills: 2. Order fractions with the same denominator (halves and quarters).
Values: 1. Express ways they use fractions on a daily basis.
Values: 2. Show enthusiasm dividing items in fractions to share with a friend.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Students create fractions given to them from playdough or paper and write the correct name of fractional parts in digits and words.
Project: Students create art projects to demonstrate fractions. Fractions hat
Fraction Flowers

Oral Presentation: Students create a model pizza and use fractions to show the toppings used on the pizza. Students will orally explain the type of pizza they made and state the fraction of ingredients used for the toppings. Students will state what fraction of the toppings is the biggest and/or smallest.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Guide students in writing the numeral for one half as ½ and one quarter as ¼
Provide students with opportunities to identify the numerator and denominator of fractions given to them.
Model reading fractions correctly.
Provide opportunities for student to compare fractions using >, <, =. For example, students can use playdough to create creations and compare their sizes physically.
Another example, students can engage in folding and cutting paper to create and compare fractions.

Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher about the Outcome: Two types of numbers are odd and even. Odd numbers are those when divided by two have a remainder of one while even numbers are those numbers that can be divided exactly in two. Odd numbers end with 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9. For example: 21, 43, 25 , 57 , 69, etc. Even numbers are those that can be divided equally by two. Even numbers end with the digits: 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8. For example, 16, 32, 54, 90, 96, 78, etc. Even numbers are a subset of Integers and not natural numbers. Zero is an even number and can be represented as two sets of nothing. An even sum can only be arrived at by adding two even addends, or any two odd addends (e.g. 2 + 4 = 6 ; 3 + 5 = 8).

Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher about the Outcome: We compare numbers by their value - the bigger the value the greater the number. Values that are the same, we use the equal sign to show their relationship. Once the values are not the same then we compare by using the symbol of the alligator where the mouth is always open to the larger quantity. It is only one sign that can be read backward or forward to change the meaning of the sign, for example, 12 < 34 can be read forward as 12 is less than 34 or backward 34 is more than 12.

Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher about the Outcome: A fraction is a part of a whole. A unit fraction is any fraction with 1 as its numerator (top number), and a whole number for the denominator (bottom number) equal sign. Each unit fraction has a name. In order to give names to fractional parts, the whole must be divided in equal parts. One half is the name given when a whole is divided into two equal parts (not one over 2), when the whole is divided into four equal parts, each part will be called one quarter (not 1 fourth).
Additional Resources and Materials

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: 1.1 Additive Thinking - Understanding the meaning of addition and subtraction and how they relate.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: 1. Add up to three two-digit numbers within 100 using a variety of strategies without and with regrouping.
2. Subtract two-digit number from two-digit number using a variety of strategies without and with regrouping.
3. Mentally demonstrate their ability to add 10’s or 100’s up to 900 from a given number.
4. Identify that addition is the opposite of subtraction.
5. Use appropriate vocabulary to explain addition and subtraction strategies.
1. Use a variety of strategies and tools to add and subtract two-digit numbers with and without regrouping.
2. Use problem solving strategies for up to two step problem involving addition and subtraction using single digits.
1. List occasions when they have to add or subtract.
2. Appreciate the importance of knowing how to add and subtract in everyday life.
Read worded problems and determine which strategy to use to solve the problem.
Students will place words correctly under the heading “Addition” or “Subtraction” to demonstrate their understanding of what the different words require.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Students demonstrate their ability to add using various strategies, or strategy that works best for them.
https://i.etsystatic.com/31572743/r/il/9403a4/3993771075/il_fullxfull.3993771075_rnbb.jpg
https://www.math-salamanders.com/image-files/addition-subtraction-worksheet-to-10-3.gif
Peer review: Allow students to hear each other in pairs, groups or whole class articulate strategies they are using for correctness to guide each other.
Online Games: Students will place words correctly under the heading “Addition” or “Subtraction” to demonstrate their understanding of what the different words require.
Students participate in an activity in which they compete with each other to quickly calculate addition and subtraction items mentally. They then determine which number sentences are correct.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Teacher provides students with opportunities to demonstrate their different strategies for adding and subtracting numbers. They can then be exposed to different strategies (e.g. place value mat, friends of ten, crossing out, bar model) and choose the ones with which they are most comfortable.

Always move from the known or simple to unknown. Some students are confused when prerequisite skills are not properly understood. This is necessary to help students who need to understand how one concept is linked to another.
- Allow students to explain how they could find the sum of 5 + 5 or any other addition fact. Provide experiences with estimation strategies using grouping of tens.
- Provide opportunities for students to see subtraction as both a counting-up procedure and a counting-down procedure. (e.g., 15 – 11 by counting up from 11 or counting down from 15).
Ask students to use logical reasoning to find basic
Addition and subtraction facts for the same number. For example, if students have the number 12, what numbers can be added to give 12? Also, what numbers when subtracted can will give 12?
Provide opportunities for students to match number sentence to a given story problem.
Allow students to model simple addition and subtraction using concrete or visual representations and record the process symbolically. E.g. 5 + 6 = 11.
Give students opportunities to use support manipulatives, develop mental strategies and develop personal strategies for adding and subtracting.

Present videos to students to help them reinforce the concept of how to add and subtract numbers. This can serve as a strategy to help weak students in a one-o-one session as it is demonstrated via another medium.
Use simple word problems initially.

Word problems are introduced to students for them to make sense of how addition and subtraction are used in the real world. They begin to understand how mathematical information can be extracted from words that can then be represented by symbols. It is important to stress the importance on representing it correctly without changing the meaning of the words.

Other Strategies
Mental Maths strategies
e.g. 1: 63 - 34 = Students can take one from 34 which gives 33. It is easy to subtract now.
63 - 33 = 30 but one more needs to be subtracted so 30 - 1 = 29
E.g. 2: 45 - 19 = Students can add one to 19 which gives 20. It is easy to subtract now. 45 - 20 = 25 but we need to add back the one we took, so 25 + 1 = 26

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: 1.2 Additive Thinking - Compute Fluently

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns.
Write an equation to express total as a sum of equal addends.

Skills
1. Demonstrate initial multiplication and division knowledge related to sharing, grouping and array.
2. Show how multiplication undo division and vice versa.
3. Finding a missing quantity given the whole and another known quantity.

Values
1. Identify times in their daily lives where they would have to use the multiplication strategy or identify totals using array.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Students are given two dice. They are asked to roll each die: one die will represent the number of columns and the other will represent the number of rows that they will make. Students will then arrange items to create arrays, then identify the total number of objects in each array. Teacher observes students to determine if they can create arrays and identify the total number of objects in each array.
Students are given objects and asked to put items in arrays based on the number given to them.
Students are given a fixed number and are asked to create arrays. (e.g., 12, 15, 18)
Students are given a number sentence to create an array.
Students create a number sentence based on the array.
Worksheets
Create a number sentence based on the picture.
Teacher presents examples of arrays and students determine how to write equations to match the arrays.

Game: Students are placed into teams. Each team is given the same number (e.g. 16) and is asked to compete to see which team can create the largest number of arrays and number sentences for the given number. The first team to do so will be the winner.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Reinforce counting in various ways: 2, 5, 10 etc. using charts to connect number of objects to numbers in the counting sequence.
Give students opportunities to separate any given quantity into equal groups or sets. E.g. Share 12 cupcakes among 4 children. How many cupcakes will each child get?
- Give students opportunities to combine groups to find the whole. E.g. If there are 4 mangoes in one bag, how many mangoes are there in 5 similar bags?
Allow students to represent array by making sets of or rows of blocks, counters, or square tiles.
Allow students to use number lines, chairs, floor tiles to make jump of as they count on the number line to find a given product.
Present a video about the concept of arrays in real life situations. After watching the video, students will identify other examples of the use of arrays in their environment.
Video link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5ae6Wn66epg

Present students with a number of objects (e.g. toy cars, sweets, counters, building blocks) Have students arrange items in arrays of their choice (12 objects can be arranged in arrays such as:
1 column,12 rows
2 columns, 6 rows
3 columns, 4 rows
Students play a game and arrange items based on the number rolled on the dice.
- Allow students to demonstrate repeated addition as a strategy for multiplication. Modelling with concrete grouping first to show equal groups using playing cards etc.

Allow students to discover through playing with concrete material that multiplication and division undo each other. E.g. 4 groups of 5 equals 20 then 20 pencils placed in groups of 4 will be equal to 5 pencils in each group.

- Provide opportunities for students to use concrete materials to find missing quantities then represent same with number sentences.
E.g. 5 x __ = 10 then, 10 ÷ 5 = __

Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher about the Outcome: Multiplication is one of the four basic Math operations. It is the opposite of division. When we multiply numbers (factors), our answer (product) gets bigger. Multiplication gives the same answer as repeated addition, that is, we add the same number/quantity a number of times (5 + 5 + 5 + 5). In writing a multiplication sum from sets, always put the number of sets first, then the number in each set. For example, 4 sets/groups of 5 will be 4 x 5 =20

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Learners will create and extend simple patterns, repeating and growing patterns.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Describe a given pattern.
Identify whether given terms form a pattern or not.
Make predictions as to what may come next in a given pattern.
Create and extend simple patterns with 3-5 elements.
Create patterns resulting from repeating an operation or making a repeated change to an attribute.
Copy and extend a repeating pattern (using manipulatives, diagrams, body movements/actions/sounds)
Translate simple repeating patterns (E.g., a repeating orientation pattern to a repeating sound pattern, etc.).
Copy and extend growing patterns.
Recognize patterns as either repeating or growing.
Acknowledge patterns in their environment by identifying and describing the pattern observed around them.
Identify and enjoy patterns in poems, stories, (folk and modern) within their culture.
Can students extend given patterns involving shapes, colour and sizes?
Students should be able to identify a pattern as growing or repeating.
Students should be able to extend growing patterns.
Can students explain the pattern rule used? e.g. 2, 4,6,8,10,
Are students able to build patterns using geometric shapes? E.g.
Johnny did this with his building blocks
Tina wrote these numbers down on her book 5, 10, 15, 20, 5, 10, 15, 20

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Gathering information through Teacher Conversation, Observation and Products
Are students able to recognize and describe patterns found in their environment? (patterns in the communities, on fabrics, patterns on a calendars).
Symbolic: Present learners with a piece of fabric with a similar design as shown below. Learners will look at the illustration to identify the rule and add on to the repetition.
Checklist: Can identify shapes and rule (described in own words), Can accurately continue the pattern
Can students demonstrate patterns through dancing, or performing actions? E.g. clap, stomp feet, click.
Observation: Have students listen to a sound pattern based on the following. Students will line up to continue the pattern by demonstrating the next correct action (term).
Student can describe pattern, Student demonstrates next term in pattern
Are students able to describe patterns given and patterns formed? E.g. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ___, ___
Concrete: Think, Pair, Share, Have students identify repeating patterns within their environment. Leaves, towels, blouses, glasses, rocks, plants, mugs, shoes, pencils
individual pattern)
pair, learners describe each other’s pattern)
presents their patterns to class and allows class to describe the pattern)
Have students discuss what they observe about this pattern. 1. Is it a repeating pattern? Circle Yes or No 2. If yes, draw the next five terms to continue the pattern.
Product Exit Slip Students will answer the following:
1. Is that a pattern? Circle Yes or No 2. What kind of pattern is it? 3. Draw what will come next.
1. Describe the pattern in your own words? 2. Is it growing or repeating? Yes or No
inclusive_learning_strategies: Take students on a field trip in the community or within their school environment and allow them to identify patterns around them. e.g. patterns with buildings, or patterns of houses or how the houses and streets are located.
Have students discuss why they believe there is a pattern or not and what is the pattern.
Provide opportunities for students to build, extend and describe patterns formed with letters, numbers and shapes.
Provide opportunities for learners to create their pattern and explain the rule formed by these patterns.
Students can create their own pattern for example they can create a pattern on a given template like the caterpillar below:
Use story books, printed fabrics, poems, chants and songs and allow pupils to identify patterns in each.
Provide opportunities for pupils to demonstrate patterns through actions, sounds.

Growing patterns: Students can be arranged into small groups where possible. Examples of activity to help students understand growing patterns can include: Begin the activity by having the first group snapping their fingers. The second group takes their turn by snapping their fingers and

then adding an action like clapping their hands. The third group repeats the first and second actions and adds a third. This pattern continues until the last group adds their own action. Now have the whole class do the entire sequence together.
Also, give pupils opportunities to build and extend patterns using geometric shapes.
Ask students to explain what is happening in the pattern below
Provide students with small coloured tiles and ask them to create what would come next in the pattern.
Provide Students with practice to develop the skill of creating pattern. For example, students will be given a worksheet to choose the group that would come next in a growing pattern.
Allow pupils to play games to explore patterns with letters, numbers or designs. For example, students can be given cut out pattern strips and asked to stick them under the correct types of patterns.
Provide students with number lines to represent patterns.
Allow students to use skip counting to extend given number pattern to 100.
Allow students to recognize and fill in missing elements in patterns.
Provide pupils with the hundreds chart and allow them to identify patterns and the rule used, for example: [Image of Grade 3: Patterns in a Hundred Chart by Anil Kumar]
Give pupils a number of patterns and allow them to identify the core of the patterns.

Provide students with opportunities to discover and translate repeating patterns with concrete materials, shapes and letters. Engage students in translating patterns - this gives pupils opportunities to rearrange given patterns in different orders. For example: [Image of geometric shapes pattern]

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcomes PR 1.2: Recognizing, Describing and Extending Patterns - Increasing and Decreasing Patterns

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Determine the rule used to extend a growing and a shrinking pattern.
Identify, describe, and create through investigation, growing and shrinking patterns (increasing and decreasing) involving addition and subtraction.
Skills: Solve simple patterns involving increasing and decreasing patterns.
Use pattern rules to extend patterns and to make predictions.
Complete patterns where elements are missing.
Values: Demonstrate the ability to obtain a rule for a number pattern by determining the relationship between given numbers.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Are students able to determine whether a pattern is growing or shrinking?

Can students demonstrate their knowledge about growing, shrinking and repeated patterns? Have students identify which pattern is the increasing / growing pattern and which is the decreasing/shrinking pattern.
Write ‘G’ if the pattern is growing or write ‘S’ if the pattern is shrinking.
Are students able to use informal language to describe rules for patterns increased and decreased?
Present learners with the following: 1. Describe the type of pattern in your own words. 2. Draw the next term (5th figure).
Are students able to create growing or shrinking patterns?
Are students able to determine the rule for a pattern that is growing or shrinking?
Can students extend given patterns using concrete materials, or written exercises?

Think, Pair, Share: Students will be provided with markers and a sheet of paper. With their peers, students will create, growing, shrinking and repeating patterns which they will write on paper. Student pairs will then display their pattern and have their classmates determine the rule for each pattern.
Are students able to recognize growing and shrinking patterns around them?
Are pupils able to increase or decrease patterns involving letters, numbers and concrete materials?
Can students increase patterns using geometric shapes?
Are students able to make predictions on the next element to be added or taken out in patterns?
Have students look at the pattern then describe what they observe.
Students will use the rule in order to solve for the missing term.

Peer Assessment: Provide opportunities for small groups or peer conversations on different patterns. After the chat, learners provide their peers with a “Thumbs Up” about something in the conversation the peer expressed well.
Practical tasks: Can students fill in missing elements in increasing and decreasing patterns?
Are students able to increase patterns using addition?
Can students decrease patterns using subtraction?
Can students identify patterns in a 100 numbers chart?
Write the missing values in each red circle.
In Providing Feedback to learners (consider the following)
Are students able to locate errors in patterns?
Are students able to complete increasing and decreasing patterns concretely and pictorially?
Can students solve simple pattern problems?
inclusive_learning_strategies: Provide students with the opportunity to
Identify which pattern is an increasing pattern and which is a decreasing pattern within their school environment or homes.

Provide students with the opportunity to determine missing terms in given increasing and decreasing patterns. e.g. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, __, __ e.g. AB, ABC, ABCD, ABCDE, e.g. 20, 18, 16, 14, __, __, __ Draw the picture that comes next in the growing pattern.
Allow students to identify and correct errors in given patterns.
Provide students with opportunities to solve and create real life problems involving repeating, growing and shrinking patterns.
Provide students with concrete and pictorial examples to find the rule, then determine the numbers that will complete each repeating pattern.
Retrieved from: https://www.megaworkbook.com/general-aptitude/pattern-recognition#growing_pattern_recognition

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcomes PR 2.1: Identify when symbols are being used as variables, and describe how they are being used.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners will be expected to: Knowledge
Identify and interpret simple number sentences using words, diagrams and symbols.
Communicate and interpret simple additive and subtractive strategies using words, diagrams and symbols.
Explain how the arrangement of numbers (commutative property) does not affect the sum/total.
Recognize that the commutative property does not apply to subtraction.
Represent algebraic expressions using variables and symbols.
Represent unknown quantities concretely, pictorially and numerically.
Simplify algebraic expression problems involving an unknown quantity in an addition and subtraction situation.
Demonstrate an appreciation for the use of symbols as a substitute for real life objects when counting and grouping items up to a hundred.
Simplify these simple algebraic expressions using one operation at a time. 2f + f, h + 4h, 5a - 2a, 6b - 4b

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Gathering information through Teacher Conversation, Observation and Products
Can students recognize that a variable may be represented by shapes or objects when finding unknown quantity?
Factor Entrance Slip
Identify and call out the items in the bowl: 2 red apples, a green apple, one banana
Think and Share: Using a variety of manipulatives such as fruits, toys, cubes, cars, crayons, pencils etc. students will think of a single letter to represent each object and share with the class.

Group Work: In groups of four, students will look at a menu and pretend what each person would love to order from the menu. The orders will be grouped together and given to the teacher. Are students able to recognize that unknown quantities can be found by joining or separating quantities given?

Playing Games: Students will play a game called “A Day in the Market.” Students will be taken on an organized field trip to the market. When called, they will begin with “In my basket, I have ..... Plus ..... Plus ...” Are students able to represent missing quantities concretely, pictorially and numerically?

Observation: Concrete. The learners are given a bag with a variety of manipulatives such as shells, crayons, stones, blocks etc. collected by the students, which they will group and record according to likeness.
Observation: Students are given a worksheet with symbolic quantities to convert into algebraic expressions.
Product Exit Slip: Match the sentence to the algebraic expressions.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Provide students with real life examples, to create number sentences. For example, reading the menu from two food restaurants as shown below, then place an order with the teacher.
Give students opportunities to write number sentences after listening to stories or problems.
Count the objects given and represent the number using only the quantity and a letter. If C stands for cherries, How many C’s are there?
Organize all like items together and record the number of each item.
Demonstrate an understanding of writing algebraic expression using symbolic quantities.
Allow students to represent missing quantities using shapes such as circles, triangles, squares and rectangles etc.
Allow students to represent unknowns using drawings and concrete materials.
Give students opportunities to represent unknowns using separating and joining situations.

Add the like items and simplify algebraic expressions. Use appropriate letters to represent the following as simplified algebraic expression: 3 fish + 5 hot dogs + 2 fish, 2 chips + 4 hot dogs + 1 chips, 4 hot dogs - 2 hotdogs
Simplify these simple algebraic expressions using one operation at a time. 2f + f, h + 4h, 5a - 2a, 6b - 4b

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcomes PR 2.2: Variables and Relationships - Understanding and representing equivalence.

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Represent numbers up to 100 in a variety of ways.
Apply the commutative property when add numbers.
Identifying equivalent monetary values of coins and bills.
Create equivalent sum of money using different coins.
Generalize whole numbers can be decomposed and composed without changing its value.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Gathering information through Observation and Products: Can students recognize that in adding, the order in which numbers are written does not affect the total? e.g. 9 + 16 = 25, 16 + 9 = 25
Are students able to justify why commutative property does not work for subtraction?
Can students formulate number sentence to represent scenarios or problems given?
Factor Entrance Slip: Given manipulatives, students will share them in two parts.
Think and Share: Given a specific number, students will break it into two parts. Students will share their results. Students will record findings by writing a number sentence.
Observation: Concrete: The learners are given a worksheet. which they will complete through decomposition
Symbolic: Check learners as they decompose and record their findings.
Games- two-digit decomposed puzzle
Create puzzles which show numbers in expanded form and in decomposed form. Allow students to match them to the corresponding whole numbers.

Group Work: Each group will be provided with a bag of fruit loops cereal as manipulatives. There will be a graphic organizer for each group. Students will need a pencil for writing as needed. Students will work on their graphic organizer together to come up with different number sentences that utilize the same variables that will be equal to each other in a different order.
Think and Share: Using pseudo money students will think of ways to repay a debt of $50 using different denominations except for a single $50 note. By a show of hands students share their findings.

Game- Coin Cruncher: Learners will be involved in a two-part interactive game online. 1. Make a total- learners will drag the coins to make the right amount 2. How much - Learners will add the coins and state the amount.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Allow students to discover through playing of games that numbers can be represented in a number of ways. E.g. using the ten and five frames to build numbers, or dominoes to represent the same number.
For example: Do these represent 5423? Different Ways To Show a Number | Representing Numbers in Different Ways | Place Value | Math 2021 Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7-v4ZLaZhw
Watch a video on composition of numbers to understand that numbers are made up of other numbers https://youtu.be/zLZNSNj5nVg
Provide students with the opportunity to decompose and compose numbers. For example, students will complete the following worksheet by finding the missing amount.
Another example, have students’ real-life example, such as use the denominations (money) to build the following amount. a. $70 b. $7 c. $30

4. Provide opportunities for learners to demonstrate commutative property by formulating five 2-digit numbers and its commutative property between 40 and 100. For example, use a graphic organizer as shown below.
5. Have learners view a video on coins and their representation. For example, Counting Coins Song for Kids | Penny, Nickel, Dime, Quarter | 2nd Grade

6. Allow students to represent unknowns using drawings and concrete materials. e.g. Cindy picked 40 mangoes and sold some to her friend Jan. Cindy now has 15 mangoes. How many mangoes did she sell to Jan? Which operation should Cindy use to find out how many mangoes she sold to Jan?

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcomes PR 2.3: Variables and Relationships - Writing Expressions and Equations

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners will be expected to: Knowledge - Demonstrate and explain what equality means using manipulatives and diagrams (0-100).
Record equalities and inequalities symbolically using the correct symbol.
Solve for missing quantities to make the equation equivalent.
Model equalities using a variety of concrete representation and record.
Appreciate the need for equating an expression to values.
Accept that an equation is a number sentence.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Gathering information through Observation and Products
Observation: Observe as students form equal amounts using the scale. Have students use manipulatives and discuss reasoning for choosing the answers given.
Symbolic Pair activity: In pairs students complete worksheet using hands on material.
Concrete: Given manipulatives allow students to complete: [MISSING ADDENDS worksheet image]
inclusive_learning_strategies: 1. Teacher guides students into the story of a class which wanted to form equal teams for a football game. Discuss the meaning of the word equal. The students explore using equal numbers for the teams and later uses a seesaw to look at equal weight. Discuss that the objects must be the same number and weight to balance off.
Have students view YouTube Video on Balancing off- https://youtu.be/C6tNqKdA6aI

2. Equal or Not Equal: Group the class into small groups. Each group needs a balance scale and a set of number sentences/equations. Have each group use the balance scale and a set of cube or counters to determine whether the number sentences are equal or not equal. 1. 4+5=9 2. 10 = 4 + 6 3. 12 + 4= 13 + 2
3. Using a Balance off Model - Provide opportunities for students to use pan balance to investigate and represent equivalent sets. [Adding on/Subtracting off diagrams]
4. Using Sectioned Circles - Allow students to shade pieces in the second circle to make it equivalent to the first. Students will then fill in the missing values. [Circle diagrams]
6. Provide students with Ten Frames to model equality. [Ten Frame diagrams]

Making a Ten Frame: 8 red apples and 6 green apples are on the tree. How many apples are on the tree? 8 red apples, 6 green apples. Take 2 apples from the six green apples to make a ten frame with red apples. Add the remaining 4 apples to the ten frame. Therefore, rewriting 8 + 6 → 10 + 4 = 14.
Provide students with the opportunity to use a double number line to show equality/inequality. Example: Show that 5 + 7 = 8 + 4.
