essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 1.1

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Describe orally and written, 2-D and 3-D shapes and/or objects and space.
Identify 2-D and 3-D shapes in the environment.
Explain the differences and similarities between and among 2-D shapes using vocabulary associated with properties of shapes.
Make comparisons between solids and plane shapes in concrete and pictorial forms using formal language.
Identify 2-D shapes as faces of 3-D shapes in pictures, objects or space from different orientations.
Classify shapes according to their properties/features and give reasons for the classification.
Create/build 2-D and 3-D shapes.
Describe 2-D and 3-D shapes found in the natural environment or in pictures.
Create 3D shapes using 2D shapes from a description given within a real world context.
Show an awareness of the properties of 2-D and 3-D shapes in the environment through explorations of multimedia games, toys, props, puzzles, worksheets and songs.
Investigate and reason about the results of transforming 2-D shapes using the appropriate vocabulary (flipping, turning).
Identify and describe the position of 2-D and 3-D shapes in relation to another shape, object or space.
SCO 7: Students will complete a table with prompts for 2D and 3D shape samples presented to them, using words or pictures: Shapes, What do I see, It looks like a......

SCO 8: Oral Assessment. Students will dip in a bag, select a picture of a shape or the shape name and engage in a round the class oral discourse entitled: “I am a _____ because” (e.g. I am a cube because I have 6 square faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices)

SCO 9: BINGO. In groups, students will play shape BINGO. A descriptive piece will be read to them and based on the 3D description, a peg/counter will be placed on the correct shape or word. The first group to fill out the word BINGO wins. Teacher will monitor students progress as the activity is done.

SCO 9: Provide students with the opportunity to make comparisons and explore 2D and 3D shapes. For example: Students are presented with riddles reflecting characteristics of 3D shapes, and are to use 2D shapes to create 3D objects or shapes. Example: - I am a concrete block used in construction with 6 rectangular faces. (cuboid) - You lick ice-cream off me on a hot sunny day. (cone) - You roll me as a die when you play board games. (cube) or Students who have difficulty visualising the read riddles, objects reflecting the descriptors of the 3D objects will be placed in a pool of shapes for them to select, or an assistive device on the computer can be used to help them create the shapes.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Observation Checklist: As students sort the items, teacher will monitor students’ conversations and interactions with the objects and record observations using the following observation checklist, then an exit slip will be completed as well: Can the students sort the items using a selected criterion? Can the students describe the attributes of the 2D or 3D objects observed based on the selected criteria (number of sides, vertices, number of faces, edges, etc.)? Did students work together collaboratively?
Exit Slip: Given the description of a shape, students will identify the shape...example. 1 curved surface and 1 flat surface: cone

Journal: Students will record in their journal by writing or drawing, how they felt about completing the scavenger hunt task or shape hunt using the following prompt: Draw or stick a picture or write one thing that shows what you learnt from the activity.
Crossword Puzzle: Students will complete a crossword puzzle with descriptive prompts for 2D and or 3D shapes.
Rubric: SCO 7
A rubric will be used to assess students as they create their 2D and 3D shapes and their justified descriptions.
Wow! Goes far beyond the task; gives great detail about 2D and 3D shapes, uses appropriate expression and model matches the description of the shape.
Spot on! Completes the task, shows understanding of 2D and or 3D shapes, meets the requirement and is accurate.
Moving Along Shows understanding of 2D and 3D shapes but makes errors, work misses details, expression is lacking, a work in progress.
Getting there, not yet! Lacks understanding, does not address the criteria for the task, model created is inappropriate, needs intervention.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Group Work: Place a collection of 2D and/or 3D shapes and or objects on tables for students. In groups, students will decide on the sorting criteria and sort the items accordingly (all cubes together, all cylinders together, etc.). When each group has completed the task, students will rotate around the class visiting the remaining groups and decide on the sorting criteria. A general class discussion will be held after each group has gone through the other groups.

SCO 2 Scavenger Hunt: Engage Students on a scavenger hunt around the school to identify 2D and/or 3 D objects and items found in the school environment. Students will record their findings using a given template or a table with shape headings (cube, cylinder, cone, square, circle, etc.).

Or: Engage students in a shape hunt at home and bring in objects reflecting 3D and 2D shapes/objects from home. Students will set up a shape corner with their exhibits with given labels to categorize those shapes.
SCO 3 and 5: Provide Students with the opportunity to describe 2D and 3D shapes.

It looks like a ..... Students will complete a table with the following prompts for 2D and or 3D shape samples presented to them, using words or pictures: Shapes, What do I see, It looks like a...... (with examples for Square, circle, pentagon, hexagon, octagon)

Shape Creation. Provide opportunities for students to create models of 2D and 3D shapes. For example, give students match sticks, popsicle sticks or other uniform objects to create 2D and 3D shapes. Allow students to present their creations and give reason for descriptions. Example: I used 4 sticks, therefore, it is a square. I used 6 sticks, therefore it is a hexagon. I used 5 sticks, therefore it is a pentagon.

SCO 8 Picture Shapes. Allow students to make real-life connections of 2D and 3D shapes by describing shape in the environment. For example, present students with multiple scenarios with pictures where they are to identify the 2D and 3D shapes embedded in those pictures and give reason for selection of those shapes.

Provide students with the opportunity to make comparisons and explore 2D and 3D shapes. For example: Students are presented with riddles reflecting characteristics of 3D shapes, and are to use 2D shapes to create 3D objects or shapes. Example: - I am a concrete block used in construction with 6 rectangular faces. (cuboid) - You lick ice-cream off me on a hot sunny day. (cone) - You roll me as a die when you play board games. (cube) or Students who have difficulty visualising the read riddles, objects reflecting the descriptors of the 3D objects will be placed in a pool of shapes for them to select, or an assistive device on the computer can be used to help them create the shapes.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 1.2

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners will be expected to: Knowledge: Identify 2D shapes on 3D objects.
Skill: Represent and construct 2D shapes and 3D objects.
Skill: Sort 2D shapes and 3D objects based on their attributes and characteristics.
Skill: Sole repeating patterns using/involving 2D shapes and 3D objects.
Skill: Create repeating patterns using/involving 2D shapes and 3D objects.
Skill: Build composite shapes/structures of 2D and 3D shapes and describe the pattern used.
Value: Choose to use 2D shapes to create 3D models using pictorial and physical representations.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Think - Pair - Share: Listen to learners as they describe shapes and observe them as they classify the various 2D shapes and 3D objects. Use a checklist to monitor learners’ behaviours below: Names the 2D or 3D shape. States examples of 2D and 3D shapes. Identifies properties of the 2D and 3D shapes.

Observation: Observe learners as they construct using different shapes, and pay attention to the choice of materials used. Listen as learners share ideas about what they have constructed. Can learners describe the process involved in completing their creation?
Product: Individual or Group work

Observe learners as they classify 2D shapes and 3D objects using materials provided to them. Question learners about reasons for the classifications. Learners can also be presented with a worksheet to classify shapes and use a colour code for similar shapes.

Individual or Group work: Have learners complete or construct repeating patterns in groups or individually. Learners can be given one or two shapes. Question learners about the patterns they have completed or constructed, and the rule used for generating the pattern. Record students’ responses as they are observed. Example: Allow students to display/ showcase and explain the patterns that they have created.

Product: Individual or Group Work Have learners create pictures/structures using 2D shapes and 3D objects and explain the shapes and combinations used in their pictures/structures. Display learners' creations and outlines they have completed. I made a dog. I used ... I made a house. I used ... Distribute work cards where learners are given the outline of shapes and they will use a combination of shapes to cover the outline. or Learners can be given exit cards that contain an outline of a shape and they will have to determine the combination of shapes used to make the outline.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Provide learners with opportunities to identify, describe and classify 2D shapes and 3D objects using concrete objects, diagrams and pictures (using examples and non examples). Learners will also discuss and share ideas about the different shapes they encounter. Example: Learners are given a picture(s) of a representation of a triangle. Some questions for discussion include: 1. What do you notice about the shape? 2. What type of shape is this? 3. Does the shape have straight sides? 4. How many sides? 5. Have you seen other shapes like this? Can you name them?

Have learners construct 2D shapes and 3D objects using different materials such as popsicle sticks, straws, elastic bands on geoboards, modelling clay and playdough. Learners share ideas about the shape they have constructed.

(google images) Allow learners to identify, classify and draw shapes with similar properties in varied sizes and different orientations. Give learners materials such as cut-outs or pictures of 2D shapes and 3D objects and allow them to sort them out. Learners can also use a colour code for the pictures.

(google images) Provide learners with opportunities to complete and construct repeating patterns using 2D shapes (tessellations). Use paper cut outs of various shapes or pictures to create/start a pattern and allow learners to complete the pattern. Use the game of ‘Show and Tell’. Give learners cut-out or pictures to create and show their own patterns and allow them to talk about the rule or pattern they created.

(Elementary and Middle School Mathematics pg. 451) Have learners explore ways of covering surfaces to build pictures/structures using 2D shapes and 3D objects. Allow learners to explore with tangrams. Give the learners puzzles where they can outline pictures/shapes using materials such as tangrams pieces, attribute blocks, pentominoes and/or pattern blocks. (google images) Have learners record and talk about their exploration of building their own pictures/structures using various materials, for example, geoboards and elastic bands to make a house. Learners are allowed to explore their creativity as they work with the materials. (google images)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 2.1

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Recognize and describe attributes and characteristics of 2D shapes (number of sides, number of corners, straight sides, curved sides) and 3D objects (number of edges, corners, rolls or slides, flat sides or curved sides, uniform thickness or not (coming to a point, curved all around)).
Classify two-dimensional figures as polygons or non-polygons.
Learners should be able to: Knowledge 1. Identify properties of 2D shapes.
2. Justify classification of 2D shapes according to attributes.
3. Identify similarities and differences between 2D shapes based on attributes such as sides or corners.
4. Identify similarities and differences between 3D objects based on attributes such as sides, edges, rolls/slides, uniform thickness or not.
5. Classify 2D shapes as polygons or non-polygons.
Skills 6. Compare 2-D shapes in the environment.
7. Differentiate between polygons and non-polygons in the environment.
Values 8. Show an awareness of the existence of 2D shapes by discussing where they are found in everyday life.
SCO #4
SCO #4: Each 3D shape will be placed on the ramp and results will be recorded in the table below. Learners will tick the appropriate column.
SCO #6, 7, 8: Give each learner four cards. He/she will name and draw a different real-life 3D object on each card and will name the 2D shapes that it is made of.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: (SCO # 1, 2, 3, 5) OBSERVATION CHECKLIST: As learners make use of geoboards and tangrams, the following checklist will be used. Learner is able to: 1. Form 2D shapes using elastic bands. 2. Identify and form polygons using 2D shapes. 3. List properties of 2D shapes. 4. List similarities among 2D shapes. 5. List differences among 2D shapes. 6. Classify 2D shapes as polygons or non-polygons.

(SCO # 1, 2, 4) SCORING RUBRIC FOR 3D MODELS: For each shape created using play dough/clay, learners will fill in the table below. Name of solid, Number of sides, Number of edges, 2D shapes that make up solid. Name of solid, Does solid - roll? - slide? (Please tick), Solid - comes to a point - is curved all around (Please tick). Scoring Rubric for 3D Models: Description - Model is an exact representation of 3D shape. All properties of shape are evident. Mark - 3
Model is almost accurately represented. Most properties (at least 2) are evident.
Model is not accurately represented. Only 1 property is evident.

THINK PAIR SHARE: Allow learners to work in small mixed-ability groups. Observe them as they generate sorting rules and sort shapes accordingly. Listen as they discuss with peers. Allow them to explain properties of shapes, as well as reasons for selected sorting rules. Encourage discussion on similarities and differences between 2D and 3D shapes.
CONCEPT MAPS: Blank concept maps can be given to learners to fill in.

GAMES (USING PICTURES / MAPS): Use pictures/maps such as the one below to generate whole class or small group discussion. Present learners with the picture/map and ask them to identify the 2D and 3D shapes. This activity can also be used to differentiate between polygons and non-polygons.

OBSERVATION: After playing game using pictures/maps, learners will be assessed individually. Learner is able to identify: - all 2D shapes in picture/map. - most 2D shapes in picture/map. - a few 2D shapes in picture/map. - no 2D shapes in picture/map.
OBSERVATION CHECKLIST: Note: The same instrument can be used for 3D shapes.
EXIT TICKETS: Give each learner four cards. He/she will name and draw a different real-life 3D object on each card and will name the 2D shapes that it is made of.
inclusive_learning_strategies: (SCO # 1, 2, 3, 5) MANIPULATIVE-GEOBOARDS: Allow learners to use geoboards (and coloured elastic bands) to represent as many 2D shapes as possible. Class discussion: Use guiding questions to have them explain reasons for forming shapes in order to discover properties. Discuss similarities and differences among 2D shapes. Using both Square and Circular Geoboards, discussion can be generated on the properties of polygons and non-polygons. SQUARE GEOBOARD
CIRCULAR GEOBOARD

(SCO # 1, 2, 3, 5) TANGRAMS: Provide learners with tangrams and have them identify and colour 2D shapes. Allow them to use puzzle pieces to create various polygons. Discuss why they are polygons. Challenge them to create their own 2D shapes using tangrams. Use tangrams to help them conceptualize the fact that shapes can be composed from other shapes, and can also be decomposed to form smaller shapes. Note: To cater for differentiated instruction, the level of difficulty of tangrams should be carefully selected based on students’ ability.

(SCO # 1, 2, 4) Guided discovery - BLINDFOLD: Provide learners the opportunity to describe features of objects which have been felt but not seen; For example, blindfold a learner and have him/her dip into a bag of 3D shapes. He/she will name and describe the object selected using the sense of touch, according to the following attributes: number of sides and edges, uniform thickness. The following can also be asked: 1. Pick a shape with five sides. 2. Pick a shape with four edges. 3. Name the 2D shapes that make up the solid. Similarities and differences between shapes will be discussed. This can be done for whole class or small group instruction. It can also be used for peer tutoring.
Learners will then use play dough/clay to create models of three 3D shapes.
Materials needed will be placed in learning centers in the classroom so learners have easy access.

Group work - GEOMETRIC SOLIDS - SORTING: Provide the opportunity for learners to engage in sorting using rules to group given 2D and 3D shapes based on attributes. Geometric solids (assorted shapes) can be used, as well as items found in their surroundings. They can place objects in clear bags to facilitate discussion.
Allow learners to categorize shapes based on their attributes. For example, pictures of shapes can be given, and these can be stuck in tables with given headings, based on attributes.
See videos below. Grade 1 Math 12.1, Sort two-dimensional shapes Sorting 3D Shapes based on their attributes

Questions such as the following can be written on cards. - Which building has rectangles and circles only? - Choose a building and name a 2D (or 3D) shape you see. - On how many buildings do you see triangles? - How many cylinders are in the picture? - Place your finger on a shape which is not a polygon. Have learners select cards and answer the question selected.

Guided discovery - ROLL OR SLIDE TEST: Connect solids or 3D shapes to real-life situations. For example, set up a ramp and use geometric solids or actual 3D shapes found in the environment to test whether or not they will roll or slide. Allow learners to explain reasons for rolling or sliding.

MATH WALK: Provide opportunities for learner to justify their thinking about involving 2D and 3D shapes. For example, take learners outdoors and have them identify and name 2D and 3D shapes that they come across and justify the reason for naming them. They can also identify examples and non examples of polygons.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 2.2

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: 1. Identify 3D shapes in the environment based on the attributes of faces, vertices, and edges.
2. Classify 3D shapes as prisms and pyramids based on its bases and lateral faces.
3. Classify 3D shapes in the environment based on the attributes of sides and corners, circular appearances.
Skills 4. Examine various 3D shapes to identify the number of vertices, edges, and faces.
Values 5. Acknowledge the importance of 3D and 2D shapes in the creation of various objects in the environment.
Identify the shapes with the apex as having one base.
Group shapes on the premise of having two identical bases.
Recognise triangular shapes all around a group of shapes.
Recognise rectangular shapes all around a group of shapes.
Correctly name the groups as prisms and pyramids.
Vertices: A vertex is the corner where edges meet.
Some examples of 3D shapes and their properties in terms of number of edges, vertices and faces.

Cone: A cone starts with a closed curve in a plane and a separate point which does not lie in the point called an apex. The starting curve and the region inside it is called the base of the cone. Sometimes the base of the cone is considered a part of the cone, and sometimes it isn’t. Either shape with or without the base can be called a cone. Some cones can be right and some can be oblique in shape. In terms of faces, edges and vertices; a cone has one curved face, one circular face, curved edges and an apex/vertex. An apex is a point that does not lie within a shape.
Cylinder: A cylinder is a tubed shaped object. It consists of two circular bases joined by a curved face. A Cylinder has two curved edges and no vertices because it is made up of curved surfaces.
Spheres: A round ball shape solid. It has no surface, edge or vertex.
Pyramid: A solid in which the base is a polygon and all lateral faces are triangles that meet at a point called an apex. They are often named based on the bases that make the pyramid.

Prism: A solid with two identical polygonal bases and whose edges are joined by rectangular faces. Prisms are often named according to the kind of polygon that makes the base of the prism. For example, a prism with a triangular base is called a triangular prism.
Some examples of 2D polygons and their properties in terms of number of corners and sides
1. Circle is round with no corners.
2. A triangle is made up of three sides and three corners.
3. A square is made up of sides of the same length and 4 corners.
4. A rectangle is made up of 4 sides in which opposite sides are equal in length and 4 corners.
5. A pentagon is made up of 5 sides and 5 corners.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Conversation – justifying reason of choices: Which Does not belong? Why? In small groups, have students complete activity sheets of crossing the shape that does not belong. Listen to discussions and reasons for choices. Provide opportunities for each group to present and explain the reasons for their choices by outlining the properties of shapes.
Example of activity sheet: Which Shape doesn’t Belong? Direction: In each row, colour the shape that doesn’t belong.

Geometry Headband Grouping Game: One student at a time will wear the geometry headband. A 3D curved surface shape will be attached to it. The student with the headband will be given the opportunity to ask the class three questions about the shape he or she is wearing in order to guess the name of his or her shape. Example: Does my shape have a vertex. How many faces does my shape have?
After guessing the type of 3D shape, the student will move to the corner designated for this type of shape. If the child guesses wrongly they will stand in the middle and try again in the end.

Observation: Listen to discussions in the different groups about the reasons for grouping shapes and the chosen name for each group of shapes? Pay attention to manipulation of shapes to identify the number of bases, and the type of lateral faces seen all around the particular shape. Provide students with the opportunity to explain to the class their findings.
Criteria for classifying shapes as pyramids or prisms: The learners were able to: Use the apex as a common characteristic
Identify the shapes with the apex as having one base.
Group shapes on the premise of having two identical bases.
Recognise triangular shapes all around a group of shapes.
Recognise rectangular shapes all around a group of shapes.
Correctly name the groups as prisms and pyramids.

(SCO 1,4) Creating Board Name Chart. Having identified the name of their shape and its properties, learners will engage in a whole class activity of creating a 3D board chart by placing their shapes on the chart next to its name and properties. Learners will engage in an extended activity of bringing in additional shapes or pictures of shapes to put on the chart.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Whole Class/Paired Group Discussion Activity

What am I? 2D Shape Naming Activity: Provide opportunities for students to devise and follow a set of properties to create shapes. For example: Each pair of students will be given a model of a geoboard and an elastic band to create the shapes based on the properties read by the teacher. Example: I have three straight sides and three corners. Can you construct me? What am I?
This activity will be repeated using properties of squares, rectangles, pentagons, hexagons, octagons.

Group Activity: Allow students to identify 3D shape in the environment based on their attributes. For example, place students in groups. Give each group three or four real life shapes with identical properties. Focus only on 3D shapes with curved faces; cone, sphere, cylinder.

Example: Group 1: marbles, cricket ball, sweet, passion fruit, Have students discuss the attributes that make their shapes similar, that is, the number of faces and shape of faces, the number of edges and vertices. Students will provide the group name for shapes with such properties. Provide each group with the opportunity to explain the properties of their shape and the name given to such shapes.
Discovery (Group Activity)

Provide students with models of cubes, cuboids, triangular prism, tetrahedrons, pentagonal prisms, hexagonal prisms, square based pyramid, pentagonal pyramid, octagonal pyramid. Have students manipulate objects and identify common characteristics of all the shapes?

Use guided questions, for example: What do you notice about the faces of the shapes? Do they have curved edges or straight edges? What is the name given to 3D shapes with straight sides? What do you think is an appropriate name for this group of 3D shapes?

In small groups, have students observe the shapes and form two groups based on common characteristics. Provide students with two papers, one with the word prism and the other with the word pyramid. Allow students to determine which group should be called pyramid and which should be called prism with reasoning. Allow students time to discover what makes each group of shapes similar. Provide students with the following activity sheets to guide discovery.

What shape do I have? Provide opportunities for the learner to make, talk about, and classify shapes and objects from their own and other cultures. For example: - plane shapes (with paper, geoboards, Logo, and so on) - models of spheres, cuboids and other prisms, cylinders, cones, and pyramids (by using playdough, for example);

Also, Learners can be placed in pairs and they will dip for a shape. Using the activity sheet below they will identify the properties of their shape. Emphasis will be placed on the bases and the classification of shape (prism or pyramid) as the basis for naming their shape. Each pair of learners will be given an opportunity to present their findings with reasoning.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 2.3

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Key Skills/ Concept: Observing, recognizing, comparing 2-D shapes, 3-D objects
Learners will be expected to: Knowledge 1. Compare shapes and objects (2D & 3D) on the basis of attributes using appropriate vocabulary.

2. Describe the relative location/position of 2D shapes and 3D objects in relation to other shapes and objects (above/below, over/under, beside/inside/outside, to the right/left of behind/in front of).
3. Describe the movements required to place an object in a particular position, relative to another object (left/right, up/down).
4. Use a variety of models, materials to create shapes.
5. Volunteer to identify objects in the environment that are in various shapes.
Check that the student: 1. Used vocabulary that identifies position. 2. Used vocabulary that matches the actual position of the object.
SCO #3 Games

inclusive_assessment_strategies: SCO #1 and #5 Exit Ticket: Students will be given a half sheet of paper. Students will be given directions as follows: 1. Write the number of sides that a rectangle has. 2. Write the number of sides that a circle has. 3. Write the number of corners that a triangle has. 4. Draw a shape with three sides. 5. Draw a shape with four equal sides. 6. Draw a shape with no corners. Peer assessment

Product: Have students program a sequence of steps on a grid that directs a digital image (or robot or classmate) to move through a maze from a starting position to a specific location in the maze, or to the exit. For example, for the

grid below, have them both give and follow directions, such as: Retrieved from: https://wordwall.net/resource/59692040/3d-shapes-aliens How might you get from go to the cuboid if you had to go around all the things in the way? If I’m at the ball and go up 5 and then left 5, what shape will I land on?
Observation As students walk around the school and identify shapes found in environment, they complete the following table
SCO #1 AND #5

SCO #1 and #4 Journaling (Group Work) In groups of 3, give each group the geometric solids - rectangular prism or a triangular prism or a square pyramid. Have students discuss how their solid is the same as another solid. Then ask them to discuss how it is different from another group’s solid.

Give each group a cone, a cylinder or a sphere or a cube and compare it to their first solid. Ensure students are using correct vocabulary. Students should record their observations in their Math journal. Have the groups display their solids and their observations.
SCO #2 Observation Listen to students as they describe the position of an object.
Checklist: The student: Used vocabulary that identifies position. Used vocabulary that matches the actual position of the object.

In pairs, each student is given a gridded paper and similar cut out shapes or small 3D solids. Students will be separated by a small screen. Student 1 will place all of his shapes or objects in the squares on his gridded paper. He then has to give Student 2 directions using appropriate vocabulary so that Student 2 can place the shape or object in the exact position as that of Student 1. Teacher will observe each pair of students noting if directions are given properly and if students are placing the shapes or objects in the correct position.
inclusive_learning_strategies: SCO # 1 and #5 Discussion

Shape Feast: Provide opportunities for Students to compare different shapes with each other. Use as many objects of different shapes as possible. Provide each student with at least two objects, and pictures of shapes from the environment. Ask students the following question: How many ways can you sort these objects into sets? Ask them to explain their reasons for their sorting each set.

Allow students to classify real-life examples of different shapes. For example, students and the class teacher will bring in snacks that are in various shapes. Students will have a shape picnic where the snacks are placed in separate serving dishes. Each student will be allowed to take two different snacks.
The students in groups will identify the shapes and describe the shapes on the basis of attributes.
The students will then compare the shapes using the attribute of number sides.
Snacks to bring in: Doritos, popcorners, sandwiches, pizza, watermelon slices, Toblerone chocolate, cookies, crackers, m and m, wafers, gummy fruit rings, fruit loops, rice cakes, cheese.

SCO #1, 2 , 3 and #5 Provide opportunities for students to use positional words to locate everyday objects in the classroom. For example, have students write clues to describe the location of one object relative to another. Encourage them to use distance and direction vocabulary to describe the object’s location (e.g., the turtle eggs are buried 20 steps to the right of the pond). Allow students to give and follow directions for moving shapes from one location to another. For example, read (or co-create with students) a story that involves shapes moving on a path. Have students draw and describe the path in the story using the vocabulary of

distance and direction. They might use a large floor grid or the grid on an interactive whiteboard to organize and sequence their directions. Also, have students write directions from a starting point (e.g., the classroom door) to a given shape in the classroom (e.g., the rectangular window). Randomly select from the collection of directions and ask students to test whether the directions lead to the shape. If they don’t, ask them how the directions could be adjusted.

SCO #1 and #4 Creating Shapes Project images of structures on a screen or look at large photos of structures. Look at photos of structures and buildings found in the neighbourhood. Show images of important buildings including where the families in the class have come from. Ask questions to consider together: What do you notice? What do you wonder? What 2D shapes do you see?

What 3D shapes do you see? Provide students with a variety of materials to explore the connections between shapes and structures such as small blocks, large building blocks and empty containers. Invite students to build with materials of their choosing on the tables or on the floor. Students may use photos of structures provided to reproduce those structures or to use as an inspiration for their own structures. In small groups, give students materials (popsicle sticks, toothpicks, playdough) to construct shapes (pentagon, hexagon, heptagon, octagon). Have students discuss what is the same and different about the shapes they have created.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 3.1

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners will be expected to Knowledge: Duplicate composite 2D and 3D shapes that are presented.
Learners will be expected to Skill: Design composite 2D
representations combining the faces of more than one 2D shape.
Create composite 3D structures using 3D objects in their environment.
Make composite representations of 2D and 3D shapes using technology.
Solve problems involving 2D and 3D shapes.
Justify the use of the shapes used to create their composite 2D and 3D shapes stating the properties of the shapes that make them suitable.
Learners create a 3D model of a structure in their community, and present to the class. In their presentation they state the 3D shapes used and the reason for using the shapes.
The learners contributed towards every step of the task.
The steps of tasks are completed.
The shapes used to create the picture are suitable.
The cut outs were pasted strategically to create the picture.
All the shapes are used.
All the shapes are positioned correctly.
The picture is recreated accurately

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Observation Checklist
Recreating Shapes Observation Checklist
larger plane shapes created.
Learner identified the shapes used to create the 2D shapes.
Learner used the cut outs of the given plane shapes to recreate the given composite shape.
Learner identified the larger solid created.
Learner identifies the solids used to create the composite 3D shapes.
Learner correctly uses the solids to recreate the composite 3D shapes.

Pair Presentation: 2D Composite Design - Criteria: Name composite shapes, The learners accurately state all the shapes incorporated in their design, The learners give reasons for using the chosen shapes in their design.

Group Work Assessment Checklist - Criteria: The learners listened while someone else was making a contribution, The learners cooperated and was able to work as a team, The learners respected the contributions made
by each other.
The learners contributed towards every step of the task.
The steps of tasks are completed.
The shapes used to create the picture are suitable.
The cut outs were pasted strategically to create the picture.
Does the model resemble the structure selected?
Do the 3D shapes used in the model match the shapes in the actual structure?
Does the learner use the properties of the shapes to justify reasons for using them?
inclusive_learning_strategies: Engage Learners in a whole class discussion, Prior to viewing a video, see link below: 1. What are the 2D shapes that you know?
Do you know how to create any 2D shapes using other 2D shapes? (Discuss)
Learners view a video on composite 2D shapes. Make New Figures - Two Dimensional / 2D Shapes from Learning Adventures

The video will be paused at different intervals to allow the learners to predict the new shape being formed. 1. What was the 2D composite shape formed and what 2D shapes were used to create them. 2. What are the new 2D shapes that you learned from viewing this video?

Have learners observe 2D shapes composed using more than one plane shape. They identify the bigger shape created and then discuss the combination of shapes used to create this shape. Examples- Pupils study the shapes below.

Example 1, Example 2 - Guided Questions: 1. What is the largest shape that you see? 2. What smaller shapes are used to create the shapes? 3. How many of each of the smaller shapes are used? 4. Can you see another way the bigger shapes could have been created?
Allow learners to manipulate given cut outs of the 2D shapes used and recreate the shapes.

Provide the opportunity for learners to observe 3D shapes composed using more than one solid shape. Example 1, Example 2 - Guiding Questions: 1. What was constructed using the 3D shapes? 2. What 3D shapes were used?

Can you think of any other 3D composite designs? What are the 3D shapes used to created these 3D composite designs?(Discuss) Allow learners to manipulate given solid shapes and recreate the 3D shapes previously studied.
Have learners view a video on how to create composite 2D shapes before they create composite shapes of their own. Do you want to build a snowman? — composing shapes | MightyOwl Math | Kindergarten
Prior to viewing the video they will be asked the following? 1. Have you ever seen a snowman? 2. What 2D shapes will you use to draw a snowman? (Pupils are encouraged to draw a sample snowman.)
The video will be paused at different points to create the opportunity for the learners to guess the shape that will be used to create the part of the snowman being described.

Just prior to the part where composite shapes will be created using triangles the learners will be asked: 1. What composite shapes can you create using triangles? At the end, the pupils are asked: 1. How many of the shapes discussed did you have? 2. Did you have any other? (The learners are encouraged to share with the class.)
Allow learners to work in pairs with a given set of plane pattern blocks manipulatives to create 3 different composite 2D shapes using the faces of the pattern blocks.
The learners will then state the shape they created, state the shapes used in their design and give reason for using these shapes.
Allow learners to work in small groups to create a 2D picture of an object. For example, a car, house, flower, robot, fish to name a few. • First the learners discuss the design of choice
Then they combine ideas to draw a sketch of the design.
The learners draw the shapes they need on paper and cut them out.
They paste the cut outs to create their design.
Some sample designs.
The learners will identify the new shape they create and name the shapes used to create their composite shape.
Engage learners in solving the following.
The Learners are engaged in a game where they must guess the object which inspired the creation of a 3D composite shape.
Firstly, the learners identify an object in the environment and create an image of its shape using solid blocks like those shown below.
Then the class is engaged in guessing the object which inspired the creation.
The student who guesses the correct object gets the next turn.
Other building blocks, play dough or molding clay can be used in the above activity.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Geometrical Thinking 3.2: Composing, Decomposing and Transforming Shapes - Deconstructing Shapes

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Explore, investigate, predict, deconstruct. Knowledge: 1. Identify 2D shapes that are part of the composition of 3D objects using footprints, making shadows, or slicing.
Skills: 2. Deconstruct 3D objects to identify the 2D shapes that constitute their formation through slicing, making shadows and footprints.
Values: 3. Volunteer to conduct a presentation on 2D shapes in the composition of 3D objects.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Think, Pair, Share: Shapes Revealed. Have students complete a recording sheet and present their findings to the class. Provide students with shapes to cut out, as it might be difficult to draw some shapes. Listen as students explain the process and possible discoveries made; example shape orientation depending on angle.

Prism Art Wall/ Prism Deconstruction Booklet. Provide opportunities for students to present and explain their artwork. Pay attention to the number of bases in each case and the number of lateral faces. While students are working, engage them in discussions. Use guided questions such as: 1. How many bases does this shape have? 2. What type of 2D shape is created by the footprint of the bases? 3. What type of shape is created by the footprint of the lateral faces of this shape? Allow students to stick their prism footprints on the prism art wall or put all their artwork together to form a Prism deconstruction booklet.

Before and After Picture Frame. Have students create a before and after picture frame by sticking the 2D shapes used to create each shape on the after side of each picture frame outline. Provide opportunities for students to display their work.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Group work - Show the Shadow Image. Create opportunity for students to explore the relationship between 3D objects and common 2D shapes. Encourage students to investigate and predict the shape of shadows cast by different 3D objects. For example: When students explore the shadow of the sphere, they will realize that it forms a circle.

Allow students to work in small groups, to explore using spheres and pyramids, with a torch or other light sources and white Bristol board to create shadows. Try to limit the number of shapes per group to two, a sphere and a type of pyramid. For example: Retrieved from: http://wondersinkindergarten.blogspot.com/2016/02/shape-shadows-exploring-2d-and-3d.html. Before students begin, have them predict what shadow an object will make. This would help to focus learners' attention on the faces of the objects. Have students choose the right shape pattern block to represent the different shadows cast by the object. Have students explore the various shadows that one object can create when it is positioned in different orientations. Ask students to consider the minimum number of shapes required to cast all the given shadows.
Guided Discovery: What 2D Shapes can I make?

Provide learners with 3D block prisms such as cubes, cuboids, triangular prisms, pentagonal, hexagonal, octagonal prisms, with activity sheets, paint and paint trays to work in pairs. Ensure that each pair of students is given two shapes. Allow students to create footprints of the bases and lateral faces of each object. Have students identify types of shapes and number of shapes used to create their prism.
Activity sheet. What 2D shapes can I make? Direction: Stamp all similar faces on the same side. Type of 2D shape: [blank], Type of 2D shape: [blank]

(SCO 1,2,) Guided Inquiry 3D Surgical Cut-Outs Allow students the opportunity to make predictions regarding the type and number of 2D shapes they will have after deconstructing their 3D shapes by slicing. Use guided questions to help activate their thinking and stimulate discussion: For example: Examine the different 3D shapes on your surgical table. Pay attention to the formation of 2D shapes after slicing. 1. What 2D shapes can you foresee after slicing? 2. How many of each shape will you be able to get? Have students use their desks as their surgical tables. Provide each child with a cone and cylinder made with white bristol board, and a pair of paper scissors. Inform students that they are going to be operating on these shapes by slicing them along their faces to see which 2D shapes they are made of. Have students color each type of shape using a different color crayon.

Useful Content Knowledge for the Teacher about the Outcome: As students are introduced to 3D shapes, they develop common misconceptions with their ability to recognize hidden faces of 3D shapes when looking at 2D perspective diagrams. Therefore, real objects need to be used as manipulatives to help them identify the basic attributes of edges, vertices and faces of shapes. However, it is crucial that students define other characteristics, such as shapes with parallel faces and start classifying shapes according to shared characteristics. One way in which they could solidify their understanding of such characteristics is through shape deconstruction. This can be done in various ways such as slicing, creating shadows and making footprints. As a result, students will need plenty of practice with disassembling three-dimensional (3D) objects so that they can study the 2D shapes used to compose these objects.
Deconstructing 3D shapes by creating shadows: Fig. 1 and Fig.2 : After Slicing
Deconstructing 3D shapes by making footprints.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Measurement 1.1: Understanding What and How We Measure - Developing an understanding of measurable attributes

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Choose and use non-standard units appropriately to measure lengths and describe the inverse relationship between the size of a unit and the number of units needed.
Explain the relationship between centimeters and meters as units of length, and use benchmarks for these units to estimate lengths
1. Explain in your own words the concept of linear measurement.
2. Differentiate among measurable attributes related to linear measurements (length, width, height, depth and distance).
3. Use vocabulary relating to each attribute (long, short, wide, narrow, deep, shallow, far, tall, near).
4. Describe the term of non-standard units.
5. Use non-standard units (units must be the same size and must be joined from end to end - no gaps)
6. Compare and order measurements using non-standard units of measurement.
7. Choose to use standard and non-standard units for the measurement of linear measurement where appropriate.

Attribute Scavenger Hunt: Create a list of locations or objects that exemplify each attribute. For example, for "long," students could find a pencil or a hallway; for "wide," they could find a table or a river.

Attribute Sorting Game: Prepare a collection of objects or picture cards that represent various attributes. Students are required to sort them based on their attributes. For example, they could sort pictures of different animals into groups of long, short, wide, and narrow. This activity encourages students to identify and categorize objects based on their attributes.

Measurement Stations: Organize different stations with measuring tools such as rulers, measuring tapes, or even their own feet. At each station, provide objects or examples that represent different attributes, and have students measure and record the corresponding attribute. For example, at the "tall" station, they could measure and record the height of different objects.

Attribute Pictionary: Divide the class into small groups. Provide each group with a set of attribute vocabulary cards. One student from each group picks a card and without speaking, must draw a representation of the attribute on the board. The group members try to guess the attribute being depicted. This activity encourages creativity and reinforces vocabulary and understanding.

Attribute Relay Race: Set up a relay race with different stations representing each attribute. For example, at the "far" station, students could throw a ball as far as they can, or at the "deep" station, they could fill a container with water as deeply as possible. Divide the class into teams, and each team member takes turns completing a task at each station. The team that finishes all the attribute tasks first wins the race.

Attribute Building Blocks: Provide students with building blocks or other construction materials. Students are instructed to build structures that represent each attribute. For example, they could build a tall tower, a long bridge, or a narrow pathway. Encourage students to use their creativity and spatial awareness while constructing and describing their creations.
Observational Checklist: Use a checklist to assess students as they use given non-standard units to measure objects in the classroom.
Estimation Skills
Measurement Using Non-Standard Units
Measurement Using Standard Units
Representation of Measurements
Variety of Measurement Tasks
Mathematical Reasoning
Neatness and Presentation

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Cloze Exercise: Complete the definition of linear measurement. Level 1: Basic Understanding - The student provides a limited or inaccurate definition of linear measurement. The response may lack essential components of linear measurement, such as the use of units or the idea of measuring length. The definition may include vague or incorrect language. Level 2: Developing Understanding - The student demonstrates a partial understanding of linear measurement. The response includes some key components of linear measurement but may lack clarity or precision. The definition may include basic concepts like measuring length or using units but may not be fully articulated. Level 3: Proficient Understanding - The student provides a clear and accurate definition of linear measurement. The response includes all the essential components of linear measurement, such as the use of units (e.g., inches, centimeters) and the concept of measuring length. The definition is well-expressed and may include examples to illustrate the concept. Scoring Guide: Level 1: 1-2 points, Level 2: 3 points, Level 3: 4 points
Circle the pictures that depict linear measurement.
Projects: Students create measurement scrapbooks to show their understanding of vocabulary related to measurement attributes: long, short, wide, narrow.

Rubric: Create a picture book that shows the different attributes of length. Create a picture book on different lengths and use the correct vocabulary to describe each picture. Criteria: Accuracy of Measurements - Needs Improvement Level 1 (3points): Several measurements are inaccurate, resulting in misleading or incorrect information. Satisfactory Level 2 (5 points): Most measurements are reasonably accurate, but some minor errors are present. Excellent Level 3 (10 points): All measurements are precise and accurate, providing reliable information. Organization of the Scrapbook Table - Needs Improvement: The scrapbook table is disorganized, with measurements and information scattered and difficult to follow. Satisfactory: The scrapbook table is somewhat organized, but improvements are needed for clarity and coherence. Excellent: The scrapbook table is well-organized, with measurements and information presented in a clear and logical manner. Creativity and Visual Appeal - Needs Improvement: The scrapbook table lacks creativity and visual elements, appearing dull and unengaging. Satisfactory: Some attempts at creativity and visual appeal are made, but more decorative elements could be added. Excellent: The scrapbook table is creatively designed, visually appealing, and includes appropriate decorations and illustrations. Use of Appropriate Units - Needs Improvement: Incorrect or inconsistent units are used throughout the scrapbook table. Satisfactory: Most units used are appropriate, but there are a few instances of inconsistency or errors. Excellent: The correct and consistent units of measurement are used for all data in the scrapbook table.

Attribute Scavenger Hunt: Create a list of locations or objects that exemplify each attribute. For example, for "long," students could find a pencil or a hallway; for "wide," they could find a table or a river.

Attribute Sorting Game: Prepare a collection of objects or picture cards that represent various attributes. Students are required to sort them based on their attributes. For example, they could sort pictures of different animals into groups of long, short, wide, and narrow. This activity encourages students to identify and categorize objects based on their attributes.

Measurement Stations: Organize different stations with measuring tools such as rulers, measuring tapes, or even their own feet. At each station, provide objects or examples that represent different attributes, and have students measure and record the corresponding attribute. For example, at the "tall" station, they could measure and record the height of different objects.

Scoring Rubric for Measurement Stations: Criteria include Accuracy of Measurements (Level 1: Inaccurate or inconsistent measurements with significant errors; Level 2: Mostly accurate measurements with a few minor errors; Level 3: Consistently precise and accurate measurements), Following Instructions (Level 1: Struggles to follow instructions at the station and requires significant assistance; Level 2: Partially follows instructions with some assistance; Level 3: Independently follows instructions accurately), Problem-Solving and Reasoning (Level 1: Demonstrates limited problem-solving skills and struggles to reason through measurement tasks; Level 2: Exhibits some problem-solving abilities and basic reasoning skills; Level 3: Applies advanced problem-solving and reasoning skills effectively), Collaboration and Communication (Level 1: Rarely engages with peers or communicates measurement findings effectively; Level 2: Collaborates with peers but may have difficulty communicating measurement findings; Level 3: Actively collaborates with peers and communicates measurement findings clearly), Overall Presentation (Level 1: Messy and disorganized presentation with little attention to detail; Level 2: A somewhat organized and neat presentation with some attention to detail; Level 3: A well-organized and visually appealing presentation with great attention to detail).

Attribute Pictionary: Divide the class into small groups. Provide each group with a set of attribute vocabulary cards. One student from each group picks a card and without speaking, must draw a representation of the attribute on the board. The group members try to guess the attribute being depicted. This activity encourages creativity and reinforces vocabulary and understanding.

Attribute Relay Race: Set up a relay race with different stations representing each attribute. For example, at the "far" station, students could throw a ball as far as they can, or at the "deep" station, they could fill a container with water as deeply as possible. Divide the class into teams, and each team member takes turns completing a task at each station. The team that finishes all the attribute tasks first wins the race.

Attribute Building Blocks: Provide students with building blocks or other construction materials. Students are instructed to build structures that represent each attribute. For example, they could build a tall tower, a long bridge, or a narrow pathway. Encourage students to use their creativity and spatial awareness while constructing and describing their creations.
Observational Checklist: Use a checklist to assess students as they use given non-standard units to measure objects in the classroom.

Students are observed using a checklist as they engage in hands-on measurement activities using non-standard units. Focus should be placed on estimation skills, accuracy in measuring, and their ability to use appropriate units. Students should be observed using a checklist as they represent their measurements in concrete, pictorial, and written forms.
Activity: Measurement Using Non-Standard Units
Criteria: Accurately Measures Objects - Measures with Consistency, Uses Non-Standard Units Correctly, Makes Few Errors in Measurements
Follows Instructions - Listens Carefully, Follows Step-by-Step Directions, Asks Clarifying Questions
Problem-Solving and Reasoning - Makes Logical Decisions, Uses Estimation Skills, Explains Reasoning Clearly
Collaboration and Communication - Works Well with Peers, Shares Ideas and Contributions, Communicates Findings Clearly
Engagement and Participation - Actively Participates, Shows Enthusiasm for the Activity, Stays Focused and On-Task

Paper and pencil: Pupils are given worksheets to complete the following: Students select appropriate non standard units to measure the length of given objects and pictorial representations, then record and compare the lengths. Students choose to use standard and non-standard units for the measurement of linear measurement where appropriate.
Example: Measuring Lengths with Non-Standard Units of Measure. Measure each object using paper clips.

Portfolio: Create a portfolio of students' work that showcases their ability to estimate, measure, and represent measurements in various ways. Include samples of their concrete models, pictorial representations, and written explanations. Evaluate their growth and progress over time and provide feedback on their strengths and areas for improvement.
Demonstrates little or no understanding of estimation.

Makes reasonable estimations for some objects, with occasional errors.
Consistently makes accurate estimations for various objects.

Proficiently uses standard units for measurement with minimal errors.
Represents measurements with little or no organization or clarity.

Represents measurements effectively, with a high level of clarity and organization.
Presents only a few types of measurement tasks.

Includes a reasonable variety of measurement tasks.
Showcases a wide range of measurement tasks, demonstrating depth and creativity.
Demonstrates minimal or no mathematical reasoning in measurement.
Makes some attempts at mathematical reasoning but lacks coherence.
Shows logical mathematical reasoning in most measurement tasks.
Demonstrates sophisticated mathematical reasoning and connections across tasks.
Portfolio is messy and lacks attention to presentation.
Some parts of the portfolio are neat and well-presented.

Portfolio is exceptionally neat, visually appealing, and carefully organized.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Discussion and Visual representation - Provide students with multiple opportunities to develop language and vocabulary about measurable attributes of objects. Allow them to use direct comparison whenever possible. (e.g. longer/shorter, heavier/lighter, etc.) Allow Students to view and discuss pictures and videos that depict linear measurement and their attributes. Allow students opportunities to predict which measurable attribute(s) of given objects can be measured. Give students opportunities to justify their answers.

Demonstration and Modelling: Provide opportunities for students to match and make other comparisons of an attribute with measuring units. (Starting with non-standard, then moving to standard units). For example:
Encourage students to measure the same object using different non-standard units and make comparisons, estimates, connections and justifications.

Field Trips: Allow students to explore their school and surrounding areas, estimate and measure the length of things in their environment and the distance between two points. Students are given the opportunity to estimate and measure length, height, and distance, using non-standard units, record and represent measurements of length, height, and distance in a variety of ways (e.g. concrete, pictorial, written) on field trips.

Investigation: Allow students to build towers using blocks and provide them with non-standard units of varying lengths. Record and compare their measurements. Discuss their findings. Make a generalization on the relationship between the size and number of units. Students are provided with toy cars, they predict which car will go the furthest. Cars are allowed to be driven, students estimate distance covered then measure and record. Students make predictions, estimate and measure the following: Is there a relationship between height and length of feet? Who has the largest/smallest head in the class? Is there a relationship between hand span and footprints?
Guided Discovery Learning: Students are provided with centimetre cubes and metre strips etc. along with guided questions so that students can understand the relationship between centimetre and metre.

Use of manipulatives and visual representation: Teacher presents visual representation of a metre through the use of videos, metre stick or rule, tapeline, papered metre and posters. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mt8VjQEo9h0. Allow students to relate the number of footsteps and hand length to a metre.
Provide students with centimeter cubes or strips that they can manipulate.
Match metre strips and centimetre cubes to lengths in the environment.
Identify lengths in the environment that represent a metre and centimetre.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Essential Learning Outcome: Measurement 1.2 :: Understanding What and How We Measure - Comparing and ordering based on measurable attributes

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Measure and draw lengths in centimetres and metres, using a measuring tool, and recognize the impact of starting at points other than zero.
1. Estimate and measure length, height, and distance, using standard units (i.e. centimetre, metre);
2. Record and represent measurements of height, and distance length using a variety of ways (e.g. concretely, pictorial, written).
3. Use a measuring tool such as a ruler or a tape measure, in both centimetres and metres to measure length, height and distances.
4. Identify the starting point of a measuring tool.
5. Recognize and use the abbreviations "cm" and "m" when measuring and recording lengths.
6. Create real life scenarios that involve measuring lengths, height and distances.
Aligns the beginning of the object with the zero point and reads the measurement at the end of the object.
Height Measurement: Measures the height of objects accurately, using the same principles as length measurement.
Width Measurement: Measures the width of objects accurately, using the same principles as length measurement.
Picture Measurement: Measures the height, length, and width of given pictures accurately, using appropriate measuring tools and units.
Recording Measurements: Records measurements neatly and correctly in the appropriate units (cm, in, or non-standard units).
Comparing Measurements: Demonstrates an understanding of comparative measurement by comparing lengths, heights, and widths of different objects or pictures.
Estimation: Makes reasonable estimates before measuring to develop a sense of measurement.

Problem Solving: Applies measurement skills to solve simple problems or real-life scenarios, such as finding the total length of multiple objects or determining the size difference between two pictures.
Peer assessment: Teacher and students create a checklist and students use this tool to assess their peers as they measure.

Open-Ended Tasks: Include open-ended tasks that allow for creativity and critical thinking. For example, ask students to measure and draw a path on a map, considering different starting points. This encourages them to think about the impact of starting points on measurements in a broader context.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Visual Representations: Use visual aids such as diagrams, charts, or number lines to illustrate the concept of measuring lengths, starting and ending points. These visuals can help students visualize the impact at different points and understand how it affects measurements.
